quick_facts.label: Conducting body
quick_facts.value: Staff Selection Commission (SSC), Government of India.

quick_facts.label: What SSC CHSL is for
quick_facts.value: SSC CHSL (Combined Higher Secondary (10+2) Level Examination) is an SSC recruitment examination for clerical and data-entry roles in Central Government ministries, departments and organisations.

quick_facts.label: Examination structure
quick_facts.value: SSC’s published CHSL scheme has two computer-based tiers: Tier-I and Tier-II. The annual notice is the controlling document for that cycle.

quick_facts.label: Tier-I pattern
quick_facts.value: In SSC’s published scheme, Tier-I has 100 objective questions for 200 marks across English Language, General Intelligence, Quantitative Aptitude and General Awareness; it is 60 minutes long, and each wrong answer carries a 0.50-mark deduction.

quick_facts.label: 2026 cycle timing
quick_facts.value: SSC’s 2026–27 tentative calendar listed CHSL 2026 advertisement in April 2026, closing in May 2026, and Tier-I in July–September 2026. These were tentative calendar dates, not a substitute for the detailed notice.

sections.heading: Purpose and roles
paragraphs: SSC CHSL is the Staff Selection Commission’s higher-secondary-level recruitment route for clerical and lower-division work in Central Government establishments. SSC describes the examination as recruiting candidates for clerical and lower-division-clerk positions in government ministries, departments and organisations.

The exact vacancies, departments, post codes, pay levels, reservation roster, functional requirements and eligibility conditions are cycle-specific. Candidates should treat the detailed notice and subsequent corrigenda for the particular CHSL year as authoritative rather than relying on a prior cycle’s vacancy mix.
bullets: Common role families associated with the examination include Lower Division Clerk (LDC), Junior Secretariat Assistant (JSA), and Data Entry Operator (DEO).

Post and department preference matters: for CHSL 2025, SSC required Tier-II candidates to submit an option-cum-preference for posts and departments before allocation; allocation was to be based on merit-cum-preference under the notice and corrigendum.

sections.heading: Exam format
paragraphs: SSC’s published CHSL scheme sets out two computer-based tiers. Tier-I is a multiple-choice screening examination. It covers English Language (Basic Knowledge), General Intelligence, Quantitative Aptitude (Basic Arithmetic Skill), and General Awareness, with 25 questions and 50 marks assigned to each part.

The published scheme also provides for Tier-II components including Mathematical Abilities, Reasoning and General Intelligence, English Language and Comprehension, General Awareness, Computer Knowledge, and a skill or typing test as applicable to the post. Candidates should confirm the exact timing, qualifying requirements, marking, and post-specific skill-test standard in the notice for their own recruitment year.

The 2025 cycle illustrates the multi-stage process: SSC conducted Tier-I in computer-based mode from November 12 through November 30, 2025, declared Tier-I results to shortlist candidates for Tier-II, and then held Tier-II in April 2026.
bullets: Tier-I: 100 questions, 200 marks, 60 minutes; 0.50 marks are deducted per incorrect answer under the published scheme.
Tier-II: includes post-linked skill/typing assessment in addition to computer-based modules.

A 2026 corrigendum confirms that a qualifying PwD candidate who is permanently unable to type because of a physical disability may seek typing-test exemption subject to the prescribed medical documentation and conditions.

sections.heading: Application process
paragraphs: Apply only through SSC’s official portal when the relevant CHSL application window is live. SSC states that One-Time Registration (OTR) is mandatory before applying for its examinations. The detailed CHSL notice then specifies the cycle’s closing date, fee rules, eligibility cut-off date, documents, examination-city choices and correction arrangements.

Do not assume that dates from an older notice remain open. For example, the 2025 CHSL correction window was changed by SSC from July 23–24, 2025 to July 25–26, 2025, showing why applicants should watch SSC notices and corrigenda rather than third-party deadline listings.
bullets: Create or verify SSC One-Time Registration before starting the application.
Read the current detailed notice before selecting posts, claiming reservation or age relaxation, or paying any fee.
Save the submitted application and monitor the SSC candidate login for examination-city, admission-certificate, answer-key, result and preference-window notices.

sections.heading: Preparation approach
paragraphs: Prepare directly against the SSC-published scheme. For Tier-I, build coverage and timed accuracy across English, reasoning, quantitative aptitude and general awareness. Because Tier-I uses objective questions and has negative marking, practice should include question selection, error reduction and full-length timed computer-based mocks—not only untimed topic drills.

For Tier-II, add regular practice in mathematical abilities, reasoning, English comprehension, general awareness and computer knowledge. Candidates targeting DEO or LDC/JSA-type posts should also begin computer typing or data-entry practice early, because the Tier-II scheme includes a post-linked skill or typing component.
bullets: Use SSC’s published syllabus/scheme as the primary checklist; compare it with the year-specific notice before finalising a study plan.
Track accuracy as well as attempts because the published Tier-I scheme deducts 0.50 marks for each incorrect response.
Practise on a computer and simulate the applicable time limit, including the four Tier-I sections within a single 60-minute session.

sections.heading: Selection outcomes and next steps
paragraphs: Progression is competitive and post-specific. In CHSL 2025, SSC used normalized Tier-I scores from a multi-shift computer-based examination to shortlist candidates for Tier-II and set separate category-wise cut-offs for different post groups, including LDC/JSA/Clerk-Cum-Typist and DEO groupings.

Clearing an examination stage alone does not guarantee appointment. SSC’s CHSL 2025 preference notice states that candidates who appeared in Tier-II had to submit post/department preferences to be considered for first-round tentative allocation or final selection, and that allocation would be made under the examination notice on a merit-cum-preference basis.
bullets: Possible outcomes include consideration for LDC/JSA and DEO-type appointments, subject to the notified vacancy, eligibility, merit, post suitability, document checks and preference rules.
Watch SSC notices after Tier-II for preference submission, tentative allocation, document-verification instructions and final-result updates.
Cut-offs and vacancy numbers change by cycle and post; they should not be treated as fixed targets or guarantees.
